author: Milind Kale
title: 24,000 Kilometers, 6 Countries, 1 Camera: The Road Trip That Changed the Way We See the World
description: Crossing six countries by road, covering nearly 24,000 kilometers, and documenting every unforgettable moment along the way.
content: There are journeys you plan. And then there are journeys that quietly rewrite who you are. When we rolled out of Mumbai before sunrise, our vehicle was packed with camera gear, drone batteries, lenses, tripods, camping essentials, and enough excitement to last a lifetime. The destination seemed almost impossible—crossing six countries by road, covering nearly 24,000 kilometers, and documenting every unforgettable moment along the way. What we didn't know then was that the greatest story wouldn't be the mountains we climbed or the borders we crossed. It would be the people we met and the lessons the road had waiting for us.

Every Road Has a Different Language: The first few days were familiar. Busy highways, crowded towns, and the constant rhythm of Indian traffic slowly faded into quiet mountain roads leading toward Nepal. As civilization became smaller, nature became larger. Towering peaks replaced buildings. Rivers carved through valleys untouched by time. Roads narrowed until they felt like ribbons hanging from cliffs. Driving became less about reaching somewhere and more about appreciating every kilometer in between. Sometimes we would stop for hours just because the light was perfect. Sometimes we drove hundreds of kilometers without taking out the camera at all. The mountains decide your schedule. You simply follow.

Nepal – Where Simplicity Feels Rich: Crossing into Nepal felt like slowing life down. People smiled without urgency. Tea stalls became conversations. Small villages welcomed strangers without questions. We spent countless evenings watching clouds move across Himalayan peaks while local children laughed around us, curious about the drones and cameras we carried. The landscapes were extraordinary, but the people made them unforgettable. The camera captured their faces. Our memories captured everything else.

Tibet – Where Silence Speaks Louder Than Words: The climb toward Tibet was unlike anything we had experienced before. The air grew thinner. The temperatures dropped. Conversations inside the vehicle became shorter as everyone quietly absorbed the endless landscapes surrounding us. The Tibetan Plateau feels almost unreal. It stretches endlessly beneath skies so vast that they make you feel incredibly small. Prayer flags fluttered in the wind. Monasteries stood against impossible mountain backdrops. The roads disappeared into horizons that seemed to have no end. Some places don't need explanation. They simply need to be experienced.

Everest Base Camp – Standing at the Edge of the Earth: No photograph can prepare you for Everest. No video truly captures its scale. Standing near Everest Base Camp is a humbling experience that reminds you how small humans really are. Every breath feels heavier. Every step requires effort. The wind carries a silence that words cannot describe. We spent hours filming the changing light over the mountains, watching shadows slowly paint the world's highest peaks. There were moments when nobody reached for a camera. We simply stood still. Some memories deserve to live only in the heart.

Beyond Borders: As the journey continued, every country revealed its own personality. New languages. New food. New traditions. New landscapes. From ancient monasteries to endless highways, from crowded cities to forgotten villages, every stop reminded us that culture changes every few hundred kilometers—but kindness rarely does. Even when conversations required gestures instead of words, people welcomed us with curiosity and warmth. Travel teaches you geography. Meeting people teaches you humanity.

The Road Was Never Easy: Social media often shows the beautiful moments. It rarely shows the difficult ones. There were days when rain refused to stop. Days when roads disappeared beneath mud and rocks. Days when border crossings took longer than expected. Days when camera batteries drained faster than they could be charged. We cleaned lenses in freezing temperatures. Protected drones from high winds. Waited hours for the perfect light only to lose it behind clouds. The road tested our patience every single day. But every challenge became another story worth telling.

One Camera, Thousands of Stories: People often ask what equipment we used. The truth is simple. The camera was never the hero. The people were. The landscapes were. The unexpected moments were. A shepherd walking across an empty valley. Monks spinning prayer wheels before sunrise. Truck drivers sharing tea beside mountain roads. Children waving from villages we'd never heard of before arriving there. The best photographs were never planned. They simply happened.

Chasing Light: Every filmmaker understands that light changes everything. Some mornings began at 4 AM, waiting for the first rays to touch distant peaks. Some evenings ended long after sunset, capturing silhouettes against glowing skies. Golden hour became our favorite companion. Clouds became our greatest obstacle. And patience became our most valuable piece of equipment. Filmmaking isn't about pressing record. It's about waiting for moments that deserve to be remembered.

The Journey Changed Our Vision: Somewhere along those 24,000 kilometers, our understanding of storytelling changed. We stopped chasing perfect compositions. We started chasing genuine emotion. We stopped trying to impress people with beautiful places. We started trying to make people feel what those places felt like. Because that's what visual storytelling is truly about. Not documenting locations. But preserving emotions.

Coming Home: Eventually, every expedition ends. The roads become familiar again. The mountains disappear behind city skylines. The camera bags return to storage. But something remains. A different perspective. A deeper appreciation for simplicity. And a reminder that the world is far bigger—and far kinder—than headlines often suggest. Twenty-four thousand kilometers. Six countries. Countless memories. One camera. And a journey that forever changed the way we see the world. Because sometimes the greatest destination isn't a place on the map. It's the person you become after taking the road less traveled.

author: Milind Kale
title: Three Months in Nagaland: Off-Road, Monsoons, Wild Food & Forgotten Roads
description: A filmmaking expedition through forgotten roads, remote villages, World War II history, and traditions that have survived generations.
content: Some stories don't begin with a map. They begin with curiosity. Three months. One region. Countless kilometers of mud, rain, broken roads, and some of the most unforgettable people we've ever met.

Nagaland wasn't just another destination for us. It became an obsession. What started as a filmmaking expedition slowly transformed into a journey through forgotten roads, remote villages, World War II history, and traditions that have survived generations. Somewhere between endless monsoons and impossible mountain trails, we found ourselves documenting a world few people ever get to experience. And by the end of it, we had enough stories to fill an entire book.

Entering Another World: The further east we travelled, the more the familiar disappeared. Roads became narrower. Mobile signals disappeared. Weather became unpredictable. And life slowed down. Near the Myanmar border, villages seemed untouched by modern chaos. People lived simply, connected deeply with nature, and carried traditions that had survived decades of change. For filmmakers, places like these are rare. Not because they're beautiful. But because they're authentic. Nothing felt staged. Everything felt real.

Following Forgotten Roads: Many of the routes we travelled had once played an important role during World War II. Long before GPS and highways existed, these mountain paths connected communities and armies moving through difficult terrain. Nature had reclaimed much of it. Dense forests covered old tracks. Vegetation had swallowed roads that history had almost forgotten. Driving through these areas felt like moving through time itself. Every bend carried stories. Every mountain held memories. And every village seemed to have legends passed down through generations.

The Monsoon Became Our Companion: If there's one thing Nagaland taught us, it's this: Rain doesn't ask for permission. For nearly three months, everything stayed wet. Clothes. Boots. Tents. Camera bags. Vehicles. Sometimes the skies opened up without warning and refused to stop for days. Mud replaced roads. Rivers crossed highways. Clouds covered mountains. Dry clothes became a luxury. Yet somehow, the rain made everything more beautiful. The forests became greener. The rivers stronger. The atmosphere cinematic. As frustrating as it was, the monsoon became part of the story.

Vehicle Breakdowns Became Normal: Adventure rarely goes according to plan. There were days when progress was measured not in kilometers but in patience. Vehicles got stuck. Tyres punctured. Winches were tested. Repairs happened in places where mechanics didn't exist. Sometimes local villagers became our rescue team. Strangers who had never met us would stop their work and help without expecting anything in return. In cities, people often pass each other without a glance. In the mountains, kindness feels different. People help because that's simply what they do.

Life Near the Myanmar Border: Life in these remote villages moved at its own pace. Days were simple. Food was shared. Stories were exchanged. People welcomed us with curiosity and warmth. Every village had its own traditions. Its own dialect. Its own way of life. The connection people shared with nature was remarkable. Nothing was wasted. Everything had purpose. It was a reminder that modern convenience isn't always the same as happiness.

Experiencing Wild Food and New Traditions: Travel teaches you to observe before you judge. Some of the local dishes and customs challenged everything we were familiar with. Including foods that are uncommon in many parts of the world. For the communities living there, these traditions have existed for generations and are deeply connected to culture, geography, and survival. Experiencing different cultures taught us something important. Understanding always comes before opinions. And respect should always come before assumptions. Because every community has stories that outsiders may never fully understand.

Filmmaking in Isolation: Creating content in Nagaland wasn't easy. Humidity constantly threatened camera equipment. Rain limited drone flights. Power sources were unreliable. Backup batteries became priceless. Gear had to be cleaned every night. Memory cards were protected like treasure. Some mornings began before sunrise. Some nights ended after midnight. And despite all the challenges, the landscapes rewarded every bit of effort. Clouds rolling over valleys. Dense forests hidden beneath mist. Tiny villages resting above mountains. Scenes that looked less like reality and more like paintings.

Writing 'Nagaland Offroad': As the weeks turned into months, we realized something. Photographs alone weren't enough. Videos alone weren't enough. There were too many stories. Too many moments. Too many lessons. That's when the idea of documenting everything properly was born. The result became Nagaland Offroad. A book written entirely by our team. Not as experts. Not as historians. But as travellers and storytellers trying to preserve memories before time erased them. Because roads change. Places evolve. And stories deserve to be remembered.

The People We Remember Most: Years later, we may forget distances. We may forget exact dates. But we will never forget the people. The family that offered us food after a difficult day. The children fascinated by drones. The elders who shared stories of the past. The villagers who helped us recover vehicles from impossible roads. The smiles. The laughter. The conversations that needed no common language. Those moments became the heart of the journey.

What Nagaland Taught Us: Nagaland taught us patience. It taught us humility. It taught us that discomfort often leads to the best stories. It taught us that the world is filled with cultures worth understanding and landscapes worth protecting. Most importantly, it reminded us that filmmaking isn't really about cameras. It's about preserving emotions. Three months. Countless roads. Constant rain. Broken vehicles. Unforgettable people. And enough memories to last a lifetime. Because sometimes the places farthest from civilization bring you closest to what truly matters.

author: Milind Kale
title: Chasing the Northern Lights: What It Really Feels Like to Film the Aurora in Russia & Iceland
description: The reality of filming the Aurora Borealis is something most photographs don't tell you. It's not about luck. It's about patience.
content: There are moments that no photograph can truly capture. Moments so surreal that even while you're standing there, you struggle to believe they're real. For us, the Northern Lights were one of those moments.

What began as a dream eventually became an obsession. Thousands of kilometers. Months of planning. Countless weather forecasts. Frozen fingers. Sleepless nights. And a single hope: To witness the sky come alive. Because the truth about filming the Aurora Borealis is something most photographs don't tell you. It's not about luck. It's about patience.
## The Dream Begins

For years, we had seen the images. Bright green ribbons dancing across black skies. Photographs so beautiful they almost looked fake. But everyone who had experienced the Aurora said the same thing: 'Pictures don't do it justice.' That sentence stayed with us. And eventually, it brought us to two of the most beautiful places on Earth—Russia and Iceland. Not just to see the Northern Lights. But to document them.
## Chasing Weather Instead of Destinations

Most travel plans revolve around locations. Aurora hunting revolves around weather. Cloud cover becomes your enemy. Moonlight becomes something you constantly calculate. Solar activity suddenly matters. And every forecast becomes an emotional rollercoaster. Some days looked promising. Only for clouds to arrive at the last moment. Some nights seemed hopeless. Only to surprise us hours later. Mother Nature makes the rules. You simply learn patience.
## Waiting in the Cold

The reality of filming the Northern Lights isn't glamorous. It means standing outside for hours in temperatures that can drop below -20°C. It means wearing layers upon layers of clothing. It means frozen hands. Fogged lenses. Dead batteries. And constantly asking yourself: 'Will tonight be the night?' Sometimes we waited for six hours without seeing anything. Sometimes the sky remained empty. And sometimes disappointment became part of the experience. But strangely, we never stopped hoping.
## Failed Attempts Became Part of the Story

There were nights when we drove hundreds of kilometers chasing clear skies. Only to return with empty memory cards. There were evenings spent setting up tripods and cameras in complete darkness, only to pack everything away again without a single frame captured. But failure taught us patience. It taught us that nature owes us nothing. And perhaps that's what makes moments like these so valuable. Because extraordinary things are never guaranteed.
## Every Piece of Equipment Matters

Filming in extreme cold is a challenge most people never think about. Batteries lose power faster. Condensation becomes dangerous. Tripods freeze. Camera buttons become difficult to operate with gloves. Even changing lenses becomes an exercise in patience. Every shot required planning. Every setting had to be carefully adjusted. Wide apertures. Long exposures. High ISO. Manual focus. And endless test shots. But in reality, none of those settings mattered as much as simply being there. Because cameras capture light. Humans capture emotion.
## And Then It Happened

After countless nights of waiting, something changed. At first, it looked like a faint cloud. Almost invisible. Then slowly, the sky began to move. Not clouds. Not fog. Movement. Green waves. Gentle at first. Then suddenly exploding across the heavens. The entire sky transformed. It danced. It shifted. It came alive. And for a few seconds, nobody touched the camera. Nobody spoke. Nobody moved. Because some moments deserve silence. Photographs can wait. Experiences cannot.
## It Doesn't Look Like The Pictures

One thing surprised us. The Aurora doesn't always appear exactly the way social media portrays it. To the naked eye, it often feels softer. More subtle. More magical. The camera reveals colors and details our eyes struggle to see. But what the camera cannot capture is movement. The feeling. The atmosphere. The silence. Standing beneath millions of stars while the sky itself performs above you feels less like watching something and more like witnessing something sacred.
## Iceland and Russia Felt Like Another Planet

Both countries offered completely different experiences. Russia brought endless snowy landscapes and untouched wilderness. Iceland offered dramatic mountains, black sand beaches, frozen waterfalls, and skies that felt infinite. Every location added its own personality to the journey. But one thing remained the same. No matter where we stood, we felt incredibly small. And strangely grateful.
## The Camera Didn't Capture Everything

The photographs are beautiful. The videos are unforgettable. But they don't show the laughter. The exhaustion. The freezing nights. The endless coffee. The conversations under the stars. The disappointment of failed attempts. Or the joy when nature finally rewarded our patience. Some memories simply live beyond photographs. And perhaps that's what makes them priceless.
## Why It Changes You Forever

The Northern Lights teach you something strange. They teach patience. Humility. And the importance of slowing down. In a world obsessed with speed, the Aurora reminds you that some things cannot be rushed. You wait. You trust. And eventually, nature decides when it's time. Standing beneath those dancing skies, surrounded by silence and stars, we realized something. We weren't just filming the Northern Lights. We were witnessing one of the greatest performances on Earth. And no matter how many photographs we captured, a part of us never wanted the night to end. Because some experiences don't simply create memories. They change the way you see the world forever. And perhaps that's why, even today, whenever we look up at the night sky, a small part of us is still chasing those lights.

author: Milind Kale
title: Filming at 19,000 Feet in -30°C: The Hardest Shoot We've Ever Done
description: At nearly 19,000 feet above sea level, surrounded by snow-covered peaks and temperatures dropping below -30°C, we weren't just fighting for the perfect shot. We were fighting nature itself.
content: Some shoots test your creativity. Some test your patience. This one tested whether we had the strength to keep going. Long before the cameras started rolling, we knew this expedition would be different.

At nearly 19,000 feet above sea level, surrounded by snow-covered peaks and temperatures dropping below -30°C, we weren't just fighting for the perfect shot. We were fighting nature itself. And nature wasn't interested in making things easy.
## At 19,000 Feet, Even Breathing Becomes Work

Nobody prepares you for thin air. Not really. You can read about altitude. You can watch videos. You can train for months. But the moment you step out and realize every breath feels incomplete, reality hits differently. Simple tasks suddenly become exhausting. Walking twenty meters with camera gear feels like running a marathon. Climbing hills becomes a challenge. Even thinking clearly requires effort. The mountains constantly remind you that you're only a visitor. And they make the rules.
## The Cold Doesn't Care About Your Plans

At -30°C, the cold feels alive. It attacks everything. Fingers lose feeling within minutes. Water bottles freeze. Boots become stiff. Tripods turn into blocks of ice. Every exposed piece of skin burns. Even speaking becomes uncomfortable. There were moments when simply standing still for too long became impossible. Yet somehow, sunrise still arrived every morning. And every sunrise reminded us why we came.
## Batteries Became More Valuable Than Gold

Nobody tells you how cruel extreme cold is to electronics. Fully charged batteries became empty in minutes. Spare batteries lived inside jackets, close to body heat. Power banks stopped working. Charging opportunities became rare. Every percentage mattered. We learned quickly that at those temperatures, electricity becomes survival. Every battery swap felt like a race against time. Every shot had to count. There were no second chances.
## The Drones Had Other Plans

As filmmakers, aerial footage is often one of our most powerful storytelling tools. But nature had different ideas. The high altitude. The thin air. The freezing temperatures. Strong winds. Everything worked against us. Sometimes the drones refused to start. Sometimes batteries couldn't generate enough power. And sometimes they simply landed unexpectedly, reminding us that technology has limits. The mountains always have the final word.
## The Lens Became Our Enemy

You walk out from a heated tent. The temperature outside is -30°C. Within seconds, your lens fogs up. Condensation appears. Moisture becomes dangerous. One mistake could destroy equipment worth lakhs. Every lens change had to be carefully planned. Every camera movement had to be deliberate. Simple things that normally take seconds suddenly became lengthy procedures. Filmmaking slowed down. And perhaps that was a blessing. Because slowing down teaches patience.
## Altitude Doesn't Just Affect The Body

It affects the mind. Headaches became common. Sleep became difficult. Appetite disappeared. Some days, exhaustion arrived before sunrise. Altitude sickness doesn't announce itself. It slowly creeps in. A slight headache. Shortness of breath. Dizziness. Fatigue. And suddenly, you realize that creativity becomes secondary. Health becomes everything. The mountain teaches humility very quickly.
## There Were Moments We Questioned Everything

Long days. Frozen nights. Sleepless hours. Equipment problems. Physical exhaustion. There were moments when everyone silently wondered the same thing. 'Why are we doing this?' Because no photograph is worth risking your life. No shot is worth ignoring your body. And yet, every morning, we packed our gear and stepped outside again. Not because it was easy. Because something inside us believed it would be worth it.
## Then Came The Light

And suddenly, all the suffering made sense. The first rays of sunlight touched distant peaks. Clouds moved beneath us like oceans. Golden light painted mountains that looked almost unreal. Silence surrounded everything. No roads. No crowds. No noise. Just nature at its most magnificent. And standing there, freezing, exhausted, breathless, we forgot every difficult moment. Because beauty has a strange way of rewarding patience.
## The Crew Became Family

Extreme conditions reveal character. Everyone was tired. Everyone was uncomfortable. Yet nobody stopped helping each other. Hot tea was shared. Extra gloves were offered. Batteries were rotated. Equipment was carried together. In places like these, teamwork becomes survival. And survival creates bonds that last long after the expedition ends.
## The Hardest Battles Are Mental

The biggest challenge wasn't the cold. Or the altitude. Or the equipment. It was the mind. Staying focused. Staying positive. Staying creative when your body wants rest. Creating art in extreme conditions requires more than technical skill. It requires belief. Belief that the shot you're waiting for is worth every ounce of discomfort. Belief that tomorrow's sunrise could be the one you'll remember forever.
## What 19,000 Feet Taught Us

Looking back, we didn't return with just footage. We returned with perspective. We learned that comfort is overrated. We learned that patience creates extraordinary moments. And we learned that nature doesn't care about schedules, deadlines, or plans. It rewards those who respect it. Because some stories aren't created inside studios. They're earned. One frozen morning. One difficult step. One breath at a time. And somewhere above 19,000 feet, surrounded by endless mountains and temperatures no camera was designed for, we discovered something beautiful. We weren't there because it was easy. We were there because stories like these deserve to be told. And some of the most unforgettable frames are hidden in places that demand everything you've got.

author: Milind Kale
title: How We Protect ₹20 Lakhs Worth of Camera Gear in the World's Harshest Conditions
description: Because great stories deserve great care. A look into managing and protecting camera equipment while filming in remote places.
content: ## Because Great Stories Deserve Great Care

People often ask us a simple question. 'How do you manage to carry and protect so much camera equipment while filming in remote places?' The answer isn't glamorous. It isn't a secret. And it certainly isn't easy.

Because the truth is, cameras don't enjoy deserts, mountains, snowstorms, monsoons, or dusty roads. But somehow, those are exactly the places we love to go. Over the years, our equipment has travelled through freezing Siberian winters, monsoon-soaked forests of Nagaland, dusty mountain passes near Everest, and countless roads where repair shops and camera stores simply don't exist. Out there, a damaged camera isn't an inconvenience. It's the end of the story. And that's why protecting our gear has become an obsession.
## Cameras Hate Extreme Conditions

Nature is beautiful. But it's ruthless. Rain doesn't care about expensive lenses. Dust doesn't care about sensors. Snow doesn't care about batteries. And humidity certainly doesn't care about electronics. When you're carrying equipment worth nearly ₹20 lakhs into the middle of nowhere, you stop treating cameras like gadgets. You start treating them like companions. Because in many ways, they are.
## Batteries Become Your Lifeline

Most people think batteries are accessories. We think they're survival. Cold weather drains them. Heat damages them. Humidity affects performance. At high altitudes, batteries become unpredictable. During expeditions, spare batteries stay close to our body heat, often inside jackets or insulated pouches. Because we've learned one thing the hard way: A dead battery doesn't just stop a camera. It can end an entire day's work. And sometimes, opportunities never come back.
## Condensation Is More Dangerous Than Rain

Rain gets all the attention. Condensation is the real enemy. Moving from freezing temperatures into warm environments creates moisture inside lenses and cameras. And moisture inside electronics is a silent killer. We've learned patience. When entering warm rooms after freezing shoots, cameras stay inside sealed bags until temperatures slowly stabilize. No shortcuts. No rushing. Because a few extra minutes can save lakhs worth of equipment.
## Silica Gel Is Worth Its Weight In Gold

Those tiny packets everyone throws away? We collect them. Hundreds of them. Silica gel lives inside our camera bags, cases, and storage compartments. It quietly absorbs moisture that would otherwise damage lenses and electronics. Simple solutions often make the biggest difference. And after years on the road, we've learned to respect the small things.
## Rain Is Inevitable

Monsoons in Nagaland taught us a valuable lesson. Eventually, everything gets wet. The question isn't how to avoid rain. It's how to prepare for it. Waterproof covers. Sealed bags. Dry boxes. Protective wraps. Microfiber cloths. Extra layers. Because no matter how carefully you plan, nature always has its own ideas. And preparation often makes the difference between continuing the journey or ending it.
## Dust Travels Everywhere

Deserts. Mountain roads. Off-road expeditions. Construction sites. Dust finds its way into everything. Especially camera sensors. Lens changes become surgical procedures. Every movement matters. Blowers, sensor cleaning kits, and protective filters travel with us everywhere. Because dust spots aren't just annoying. They're reminders that nature always wins if you stop paying attention.
## Transport Cases Are Non-Negotiable

People often underestimate what happens between destinations. Flights. Road trips. Off-road trails. Unexpected bumps. Dropped bags. Hard cases have saved our equipment countless times. Foam inserts. Custom compartments. Shock resistance. Weather sealing. They're heavy. They're bulky. But they've protected memories that simply cannot be recreated. Because equipment can be replaced. Moments cannot.
## Drones Require Special Attention

Drones are some of the most incredible tools we own. And some of the most fragile. Cold temperatures affect batteries. Wind changes stability. Altitude impacts performance. Humidity creates risks. After every flight, inspections become routine. Propellers. Motors. Sensors. Connections. Because losing a drone isn't just expensive. It's losing perspectives that only the sky can offer.
## Memory Cards Are Tiny Time Machines

A memory card costs a few thousand rupees. But what's inside can be priceless. Thousands of kilometers. Months of work. Unrepeatable moments. That's why backups happen obsessively. One copy is never enough. Multiple drives. Separate locations. Cloud storage whenever possible. Redundancy isn't paranoia. It's responsibility. Because photographs can never be recreated.
## Cleaning Never Ends

At the end of every shoot, the work isn't over. It's just beginning. Lenses are cleaned. Sensors inspected. Batteries charged. Cards backed up. Cases organized. Equipment dried. Nothing gets ignored. Because tomorrow's sunrise doesn't care whether you're tired. It simply arrives. And your gear must be ready.
## Gear Deserves Respect

People see cameras. We see years of stories. We see frozen mornings in Iceland. Dusty roads near Everest. Rain-soaked nights in Nagaland. Northern Lights above Russia. Sunrises that lasted seconds. Memories that will outlive us. Every scratch tells a story. Every dent reminds us of an adventure. Every piece of equipment carries memories far beyond its price tag.
## Because Stories Matter

At the end of the day, we don't protect our equipment because it's expensive. We protect it because of what it allows us to do. To document. To explore. To preserve moments that would otherwise disappear forever. Twenty lakhs worth of gear. Thousands of hours. Millions of memories. And one responsibility. To make sure the story survives. Because cameras don't create memories. People do. Cameras simply help us hold on to them.
